mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses the nature and scope of engineering (B), including engineering as a profession with education, training, and professional associations (B.1), and branches spanning civil, mechanical, electrical, chemical, and aerospace engineering (B.2). The historical trace from ancient machines through the Industrial Revolution connects to the history of the technological sciences (A).

Directly covers the nature and scope of agricultural sciences (C), including the history from Neolithic domestication through industrial agriculture (C.1), and subdivisions encompassing crop and livestock production, agricultural automation, and farm economics (C.2).

Mechanical engineering as a branch applying physics and materials science to the design and manufacture of mechanical systems, spanning thermodynamics, dynamics, and mechatronics, is explicitly listed under branches of engineering (B.2).

Robotics as an interdisciplinary field integrating AI, mechanical structures, and control systems exemplifies recently developed interscience disciplines (D). Biomimicry of natural models for engineering problems (D.1) and systems integration with operations research (D.2) are directly relevant.

AI systems performing learning, reasoning, and perception connect to bionics as the use of the human brain as a model for computers and information-processing devices (D.1.b), and to systems engineering and operations research through optimization and planning (D.2).

The history of computing from mechanical calculators to microprocessors connects to the history of the technological sciences (A), and the computer as a programmable machine modeled partly on information-processing concepts connects to the human brain as a model for computers (D.1.b).

Computer science as the study of computation and automation, spanning algorithms, AI, and networks, constitutes a recently developed interscience discipline (D). Neural network and machine learning research connects to natural neural networks as models for electronic circuits (D.1.b), and algorithmic optimization connects to systems engineering and operations research (D.2).
Electronics as a discipline governing transistors, diodes, and integrated circuits directly falls under electrical and electronics engineering as a branch of engineering (B.2).
Soil science as the study of soil formation, classification, and function as a plant-growth medium is explicitly listed as a subdivision of the agricultural sciences (C.2).

Animal husbandry as the agricultural management of livestock for meat, milk, fibre, and labour, encompassing feeding, breeding, and health management, falls under animal production as a subdivision of the agricultural sciences (C.2).
Directly covers the history of the agricultural sciences (C.1), tracing from Neolithic origins through the British Agricultural Revolution, mechanization, and the Green Revolution.

Maize as the world's highest-volume grain crop, with coverage of domestication, genetic modification, and production practices, falls under plant production as a subdivision of the agricultural sciences (C.2).

Directly addresses the history of the technological sciences (A), tracing from Stone Age tool-making through metallurgy, the Industrial Revolutions, and 20th-21st-century computing and communication technologies.

Telecommunications as the transmission of information via electrical or electronic means, spanning telegraph, telephone, radio, and internet, connects to electrical and electronics engineering as a branch of engineering (B.2).

Edison's paradigm of industrial research laboratories and inventions spanning electric power, the phonograph, and motion pictures connect to the history of the technological sciences (A) and electrical engineering (B.2).

Tesla's design of the AC induction motor and polyphase power system, foundational to modern electrical distribution, connects to the history of the technological sciences (A) and electrical engineering (B.2).

Ford's pioneering of mass automobile production through the moving assembly line and Fordism represents a landmark in the history of the technological sciences (A), linking manufacturing innovation to economic and social change.

The concept of machines from simple mechanisms through complex mechanical systems connects to the history of the technological sciences (A) and to mechanical engineering as a branch of engineering (B.2).

Manufacturing as the transformation of raw materials into finished goods, spanning from prehistoric handicraft through the Industrial Revolutions to modern high-tech production, connects to the history of the technological sciences (A) and to engineering branches including mechanical and chemical engineering (B.2).
Semiconductors as the basis of diodes, transistors, and integrated circuits are foundational to electrical and electronics engineering (B.2).

Faraday's discovery of electromagnetic induction, electrolysis, and the foundation for electric motor technology connect to the history of the technological sciences (A) and electrical engineering (B.2).

Food production systems including industrial agriculture, processing, and food security connect to plant and animal production and agricultural economics and management as subdivisions of the agricultural sciences (C.2).
Biochemistry's applications in agriculture including soil chemistry, pest control, and nutrition connect to soil science and plant production as subdivisions of the agricultural sciences (C.2).

Shen Kuo's Dream Pool Essays recorded innovations including the magnetic compass, movable-type printing, and canal pound locks, representing contributions to the history of the technological sciences (A) from medieval China.

The prehistoric development of tools from stone and bone through metalworking and mechanization traces the earliest history of the technological sciences (A), foundational to all subsequent engineering.
Paper's invention in Han dynasty China and its subsequent chemical and mechanical pulping processes represent a significant technological development in the history of the technological sciences (A).
Silicon as the dominant semiconductor material for transistors, integrated circuits, and solar cells is foundational to electrical and electronics engineering (B.2).
Electric batteries as electrochemical devices spanning from miniature cells to grid storage connect to electrical and electronics engineering (B.2).

Leonardo's systematic investigations of mechanical devices, engineering projects, and hydrodynamics in his notebooks represent Renaissance contributions to the history of the technological sciences (A).
Textiles as one of the oldest industries, spanning from ancient weaving to industrial chemistry and synthetic fibre production, connect to the history of the technological sciences (A).
Radar as a technology using radio waves for detection and ranging, with applications in air traffic control and autonomous vehicles, connects to electrical and electronics engineering (B.2).
